data.title: Baytex Energy: Strategy Change For Better Valuation
data.description:
Baytex Energy (BTE) announced the sale of the Eagle Ford assets in an attempt to refocus the company as a Canadian company with some high growth possibilities. Management had long expressed dissatisfaction with the action of the stock price since the acquisition of the Eagle Ford properties. Therefore, a change in strategic direction was indicated. The sale price is $2.3 billion (or C$3.25 billion). This will enable a paydown of the bank line and the 2030 bonds. Supposedly, it will improve some key ratios as well to result in a strengthened financial position.

Last Article

This sale represents a significant strategy change that was followed as late as the last earnings report covered in the last article.

This strategy change likely came about due to the action of the stock price combined with the "too slow" growth of free cash flow.

With the bargaining between Canada and the United States marked by threats of tariffs and trade wars, there was some insulation of earnings due to the considerable presence in the United States of Eagle ford light oil production. But that advantage is now being sold.

Stock Price

Evidently the market is receptive to the idea as shown below:

There will still be one set of bonds outstanding. But so far as shown above, the market is more than ok with a simple structure that results from the sale. It may be actually a lot easier for the market to understand.

Corporate Breakeven Decline

The remaining heavy oil business is very profitable in the current climate. The very low-cost Clearwater production is leading the drop in the corporate breakeven of the remaining production:

(Note: Baytex Energy is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

The issue here has long been that the heavy oil earnings are extremely volatile. Because the "curve" for heavy oil is relatively steep, there is the possibility of negative cash flow during cyclical downturns.

What may make this strategy change work is the technology advances that have enabled some very low breakeven costs to proliferate throughout the heavy oil industry. This may enable decent cash flow going forward for heavy oil production even when the discount to light oil widens. That was clearly not the case in the recent past. But there is enough cost history for the new heavy oil technology to enable a decision like this.

Further insulating heavy oil from a widening discount is the recent startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion pipeline that was previously reported. This has provided yet another heavy oil route to potential markets that has firmed up demand for the product for the time being. It is likely a few years into the future before the discount to light oil returns to conditions that existed before the startup of the pipeline.

The corporate breakeven that is shown above after the sale is significantly below the average corporate breakeven that has been steadily reported by the Dallas Federal Reserve when it surveys the United States industry. That part of the survey is generally the first quarter of each year. That lower breakeven could turn out to be a competitive advantage.

Heavy Oil Business

Now when this company shows a breakeven in terms of WTI, there is an assumed discount to the WTI for the selling price of heavy oil that may or may not turn out to be the case for the time period considered by an investor. This is an extra risk that generally does not exist for light oil.

There is a fair amount of existing heavy oil production with a breakeven point that is higher than what is shown. The new technology that has dropped the breakeven point (particularly at Clearwater) has not been around all that long.

The big issue is in the fine print at the bottom of the slide. If the WTI is at $48 as shown above, then the discount is assumed to be $12.50. Therefore, Baytex is selling the product at breakeven at a $35.50 price per barrel. That is indeed an impressive breakeven for any oil product. But it also shows the danger if the discount widens during a time of weak pricing.

Pembina Duvernay

It will likely be important to grow the Pembina Duvernay light oil production because of the threat of heavy oil negative cash flows (and shut-in production) during times of cyclical downturns.

There also appears to be a few years to establish a robust Canadian light oil business to provide sufficient cash flow during cyclical downturns if that is needed.

The key to the success of this plan will be producing enough free cash flow to satisfy the market. That was not happening the way the company currently is. Therefore, management decided to materially refocus the company in the hopes of a faster market revaluation of the stock.

The business that will grow on the light oil side is shown above because the Viking light oil business is considered mature and a cash flow situation that provides cash for investment elsewhere.

Summary

Baytex Energy is simplifying the debt structure to one issue of bonds outstanding.

The only thing remaining after the sale of the Eagle Ford Properties should be the 2032 bonds. Notice that those bonds are in United States dollars. But going forward the production after the divestiture will be in Canadian dollars. There could therefore be some currency related changes to the remaining bonds.

That situation combined with the remaining production is supposed to provide a greater free cash flow yield. I personally would not mind at all seeing more debt repaid with the proceeds since the remaining production is heavy oil oriented.

In the current environment, the remaining production is very profitable. Depending upon management actions in the future, this could turn out to be a very interesting way to go.

Baytex Energy remains a strong buy idea. I personally believe that the Eagle Ford sale was induced by management impatience with the market. My suspicion was that the Eagle Ford properties were just getting to the point where they would produce as planned when this sale occurred.

It could also mean that management of the production was not working out as planned. Management was getting frustrated. So, the company was refocused upon what management knew best which was the Canadian oil business.

Logically, the new strategy could work as well. But we are also in a time period where the market believes that there will be a recession in the near term. That could mean that this strategy, like the strategy before it, needs to wait for a cyclical recovery for the value that is envisioned to be realized.

No matter how this goes though, it does point to the fact that management is working towards a higher stock price.

Risk

The additional risk in the heavy oil business is that the discount widens during a time of weak commodity prices to the point where heavy oil production needs to be shut in. It has been discussed that the new technology will enable at least some heavy oil production to cash flow in a cyclical downturn "no matter what". But that idea has yet to be actually tried in real industry conditions.

Future commodity prices are extremely low visibility and very volatile. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the outlook of the company.

The loss of key personnel could materially set back the future of the company.

data.title: Tesla's Future Rests On Energy And Experimental Segments As Auto Growth Fades
data.description:
With shareholder approval of the 2025 Performance Award package (page 58) for CEO Elon Musk, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is targeting another decade of extreme growth with the ultimate goal of being valued at $8.5+ trillion by 2035. To do so, Tesla will need to continue expanding existing auto and energy generation & storage segments, while moving projects like Robotaxi, Semi, and Optimus from experimental pilots to multi-billion dollar businesses.

In this article we’ll do a short review of the auto segment of the business to understand how sales could evolve over the next decade, look at the growth of the EG&S segment, and map out how Tesla might (or might not) be able to hit EBITDA and market cap targets laid out in the 2025 Performance Incentive Package.

Tesla Auto Segment

In brief, without a significant new catalyst to boost demand or unlock new markets, Tesla’s auto segment is likely to continue to plateau and see limited growth beyond secular EV market share trends as competition continues to increase.

U.S. EV Market Share

Tesla’s U.S. EV market share ticked up in Q2, but competitors continue to pick up ground since 2022 and are making progress on dozens of new EVs for consumers to choose from in the next three years, especially in the U.S.

China EV Sales and Market Share

Tesla’s EV market share in China is down from 7% last year to 5.5%, facing intense competition from BYD and other manufacturers in the world’s largest EV market.

Accordingly, Tesla’s auto sales in China have plateaued, after peaking in Q4 2022.

Tesla Auto Sales Growth: U.S., China, ROW

Increased U.S. sales have not been enough to offset a down year so far in China and a terrible year in the rest of the world, where Elon’s political activity seems to have had a greater and more lasting impact than in the U.S.

The 2025 Performance Award

The 2025 Performance Award is modeled after Musk’s previous compensation packages from 2012 and 2018. The 2012 package included milestones like initial production of the Model X and Model 3, 30% gross margins, and production of over 300,000 vehicles.

The 2018 package (page 8) was based on reaching 12 market cap milestones ($100 billion to $650 billion), paired with any of 16 operational milestones based on graduated revenue ($20 billion to $125 billion), and adjusted EBITDA ($1.5 billion to $14 billion) targets. All of these milestones have been achieved, with Tesla surpassing the $650 billion market cap goal as early as July 2021, paired with revenue of more than $80 billion and adjusted EBITDA of more than $19 billion by 2022.

The 2025 Performance Award targets a Tesla market cap of $2 trillion to $8.5, adjusted EBITDA of $50 billion to $400 billion, and four production goals: 20 million Tesla vehicles delivered, 10 million active FSD subscriptions, 1 million bots delivered, 1 million robotaxis in commercial operation. Musk has 10 years to achieve these targets, using any pairing of market cap and EBITDA/operational milestones for each of the 12 tranches.

EBITDA growth from increasing auto and EG&S revenue will be enough to push Tesla to the first $50 billion target, but only by 2034 based on my model.

With a 65% EBITDA/Net Income ratio and 3.9 billion fully diluted shares, only a P/E ratio of 200 or more can push Tesla past even the halfway market cap milestone ($4.5 trillion). At a relatively more reasonable P/E of 50, Tesla would not even achieve the first ($2 trillion) milestone by 2035.

This further indicates that significant material EBITDA will need to come from currently “experimental” business segments: Tesla Semi, Robotaxi, and Optimus.

A lot can change in 10 years, and while none of these are ready to scale today, Tesla will certainly have the profit to pour into R&D for all three business lines for the foreseeable future. But achieving EBITDA targets up to $400 billion will require pretty tremendous sustained uptake for some or all of these products, which highlights the biggest risk for Tesla over the next decade.

When Tesla was just an electric car company, it was genuinely doing something competitors were not; at release, Roadster, Model S, and arguably Model X and Model 3 were unmatched in the auto market.

Now, however, there are serious, well-funded competitors pursuing electric semis, autonomous rideshare, and humanoid robot production. Tesla has manufacturing scale and capital, but a blue ocean of demand is likely to turn red quickly for these product lines. Based on the plateau emerging in auto sales as EV market competition heats up, it’s not a foregone conclusion that Tesla can reproduce the historic growth of the past.

I do think that all of these milestones are achievable over the next decade. But I believe they will be much more challenging, and present much greater risk of failure, than the previous performance awards.

Risks

Bulls:

While all of Tesla’s experimental segments show some signs of progress, widespread adoption for any of the three are not guaranteed. If competitors beat Tesla by meaningful margins on price or performance in electric semis, autonomous rideshare, or humanoid robots, Tesla may fail to capture enough market share to achieve lofty EBITDA targets.

I am particularly skeptical of Optimus during the next decade. I still don’t see a product-market fit where robots like this are a slam dunk, even if some niche use cases have emerged. Initial cost, maintenance, and relative inefficiency of motion compared to biological workers lead me to believe this will be the worst performing experimental segment through 2035, even as it generates the most hype.

Bears:

Tesla has done what seemed impossible to many several times before. In particular, Robotaxi service presents a real opportunity to absorb a meaningful portion of the $60 billion rideshare market, which will only continue to grow. Tesla has a relatively clear path to success here, even if it will take longer than claimed, and the addition of this revenue alone could push the company past EBITDA and market cap targets.

Competition for the EG&S segment for Tesla is still surprisingly subdued. This part of the business could easily grow faster than projected as the BESS market continues to expand, and could make up for missed cashflow from the experimental segments.

Conclusion

Tesla is in an interesting position today, where the market price is largely supported by a pretty astronomical P/E ratio based on hype about future products, particularly Robotaxi and Optimus. Meanwhile, the auto business has stagnated for three years, and EG&S, while growing quickly, still needs years more to become a real driver of EBITDA and market cap expansion.

How long can the P/E shenanigans continue? One could argue that if the first half of 2025 didn’t sour Tesla (or Elon) fanatics, nothing really will and investors that have pushed Tesla up this far can go on overpaying for the business indefinitely.

Alternatively, one could make the argument that eventually P/E simply must fall to more realistic levels, even if that means a ratio of 30-50. That would put 2025 prices into the $120-$150 range, which seems more sensible to me given the current scale and uncertainty around future growth overperformance matching past results. But I suspect those prices would look like a screaming deal to many Tesla diehards, who would in turn drive prices back up.

The 2025 Performance Award helps give us a good roadmap for where Tesla intends to head over the next decade. The milestones are lofty, and will require very strong execution against fierce competition in a way previous Performance Awards did not. But based on my models above, and what I think is possible in the Robotaxi and semi markets, I think Elon could be on his way to a trillion dollar payday in the 2030s.

As early indicators, keep an eye on EG&S revenue growth and auto sales that can at least keep up with global EV market share growth. I rate Tesla a Hold, too expensive to be buying now but too promising to bet against without signs of slowing growth. Let’s see what the future holds!

data.title: Ligand Pharmaceuticals: Superb Q3/2025 With Second Guidance Raise Heralds Good Things To Come
data.description:
This is my tenth Ligand (LGND) article, following my most recent 06/2025 "Ligand Pharmaceuticals: Attractive Royalty Model Powers Forward". In Powers Forward, I rated Ligand as a "Buy". It has since risen big, ~75%. In this article, I will assess its future investment prospects.

Ligand's Q3/2025 earnings reported top and bottom line beats plus second guidance raise.

Ligand treated its shareholders to a superb Q3/2025 report before market opening on 11/06/2025. Its top line revenue came in at $115.46 million (+122.9% Y/Y) a beat of expectations by $57.41 million. Its bottom line GAAP EPS of $5.68 beat expectations by $4.63.

The cherry on top was its second guidance raise for 2025; its previous guidance called for:

  1. total core revenues of $200 million to $225 million which it raised to $225 million - $235 million, an increase of its midpoint of >8%;
  2. core adjusted earnings per diluted share of $6.70 to $7.00 which it raised to $7.40 to $7.65, an increase of ~10%.

Its earnings press release reported the following drivers to its ~68% increase in revenues for its Q1-Q3/2025 revenues compared to its Q1-Q3/2024 revenues:

  1. $36.5 million in higher royalty revenue;
  2. $24.5 million income associated with the out-license of Zelsuvmi; and
  3. $28.6 million gain on the sale of its Pelthos business.

Ligand has ~$1 billion in deployable liquidity available for use in generating new acquisition deals which sets the groundwork for future growth. As noted by CEO Davis during Ligand's Q3/2025 conference Call Ligand ended:

... the quarter with a strong balance sheet, including approximately $1 billion in deployable capital, factoring in our undrawn credit facility, which will allow us to take advantage of our robust business development pipeline.

This development pipeline is the secret sauce that makes Ligand such an attractive investment. Its experienced deal teams are constantly on the lookout for attractive opportunities. During the Call Davis advised:

...what truly differentiates Ligand as a royalty aggregator is the expertise of our deal team in sourcing and executing high-quality investment opportunities and the ability to drive superior returns with our operating capabilities and our special situations initiatives.

Ligand's diverse portfolio of royalty producing assets is primed for long term growth.

Ligand's website accessed 11/08/2025 describes its approach to creating value for shareholders as the identification of:

...high-value, risk-mitigated biopharmaceutical assets. We take a systematic and disciplined approach to investing to create a diversified portfolio of programs that produce consistent and predictable revenue streams for our shareholders.

It breaks this general approach down into four specific categories:

  1. Royalty Monetization — acquisition of biopharmaceutical royalty producing assets from a variety of sources, currently consisting of more than 90 programs;
  2. M&A — acquisition of companies with under developed pharma assets that Ligand can help to achieve their full potential;
  3. Project Finance — finance of late stage programs in return for royalties;
  4. Platform Technology Acquisition — acquisition of scalable platforms with broad applicability.

Ligand's 10-K includes the graphic below further describing these approaches to securing late stage assets to its portfolio:

Focusing on its nine months' total royalty columns for 2024 of ~$74 million compared to nine months' total for 2025 of $110 million comes to a robust ~50% increase. Kyprolis, its top producing asset subtracted ~$2 million from this growth. Evomela, Teriparatide injection and Rylaze were also laggards. Filspari, Vaxneuvance, Ohtuvayre, and Capvaxive more than made up for the laggards.

Of its eight products whose royalties are separately listed four showed reduced nine month growth, while four showed growth; of these latter four, Filspari with its nine month 2025 revenues of ~$21 million compared to 2024's ~$7.4 million was champion by a good margin.

FILSPARI, Ligand's number two revenue generator represents an enticing opportunity. It is currently FDA approved to treat IgA nephropathy in adults. Its current owner Travere Therapeutics has an sNDA pending for it in treatment of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. The FDA has granted it a PDUFA date of 01/13/2026 and has indicated that it is currently planning to hold an advisory committee meeting.

If FILSPARI gains approval in treatment of FSGS it could boost its peak sales potential to mega blockbuster status; according to a Guggenheim analyst's update:

... investors underappreciate the FSGS commercial opportunity, which is substantially larger than that for IgAN. This is based on a higher unmet need for effective FSGS drug therapies since patients progress to kidney failure at twice the rate as IgAN patients, and there are fewer expected competitors reducing price sensitivity. Guggenheim has projected $2 Billion in peak sales for Filspari in FSGS.

During Ligand's Q3/2025 conference Call CEO Davis gave a more restrained take on the FSGS opportunity when he noted:

If approved, the FSGS indication could significantly expand FILSPARI's market opportunity, potentially north of $1 billion in FSGS alone according to sell-side analysts.

Regardless, FILSPARI has potential to be a huge growth driver for Ligand with its 9% royalty. In addition to its table of disaggregated revenues as shown above, the 10-Q publishes the following table which allows investors to further drill down into Ligand's royalty revenue streams:

FILSPARI's (sparsentan) unassuming deep background described below illustrates a particular attraction of Ligand:

  1. it was developed by Pharmacopeia which was acquired by Ligand in 2008;
  2. in 2012 Ligand licensed its Dual Acting Receptor Antagonist of Angiotensin and Endothelin receptors program [DARA] to Martin Shkreli's Retrophin in 2012 in return for $1 million out front, >$75 million in milestones as well as 9% in royalties on potential future worldwide sales by Retrophin;
  3. In 2012 Retrophin changed its name to Travere Therapeutics (TVTX).

Ligand has a deep bench of molecules either currently generating revenues or in development. These include:

  1. 12 Key Commercial Products;
  2. 8 Key Pipeline Products; and
  3. a grabbag of some 35 "other products" in various states of development.

FILSPARI with its potential to upend Ligand's future income stream has been percolating along for decades. Who would have predicted its current prominence? How many other such success stories lie unheralded in Ligand's future.

Conclusion

Despite Ligand's recent growth spurt I still rate it a Buy. I expect Ligand's superior business model and execution to continue support its ongoing growth. That is not to say that it will grow without pullbacks. As noted earlier Kyprolis, its current lead commercial product is struggling.

If this would continue and accelerate at a time when FILSPARI was also facing challenges, say if its FSGS got a CRL, Ligand could pull back strongly. However the breadth and diversity of its revenue producing assets assures that any such rough patch would be limited.

The excerpt below from Ligand's Q3/2025 earnings Presentation slide 3 describes its strategic differentiation; it includes its expectation of long–term royalties to meet or exceed 22% CAGR:

data.title: Propel Holdings: Don't Ignore The Attractive GARP Setup After The Selloff
data.description:
All figures are listed in $USD unless otherwise noted.

All financial data is from Seeking Alpha unless otherwise noted.

Investment Thesis

Propel Holdings Inc. (PRL:CA)(OTCPK:PRLPF) provides installment loans and lines of credit to subprime borrowers. The company's Q3/2025 earnings, along with forward guidance, has driven the share price down ~12.6% since they reported earnings. The reaction to the Q3/2025 earnings was overblown, and Propel now presents an attractive entry point when looking at the relative valuation and the overall strategy, which leads to huge GARP potential.

Attractive Relative Valuation

Over the past year, Propel has traded at an average forward P/E ratio of 13.1x, and the current share price indicates a forward P/E ratio of 8.6x.

With revenue and earnings growth slowing over the past year relative to expectations, a pullback in the P/E multiple makes sense. That said, we think Propel offers attractive value here. The stock trades at a low earnings multiple compared to its growth profile, which is reflected in the PEG ratio.

Our bullish stance centers on the runway Propel has as it evolves into a more established lender. Looking across the subprime lending space, mature players like goeasy (GSY:CA) and Upstart Holdings (UPST) typically trade around 8x–12x earnings. Propel’s current multiple is already near the low end of that range, yet the company is still posting growth more in line with an early-stage operator.

In its Q3 results, Propel adjusted its FY2025 guidance, calling for loan balance growth of roughly 20%, revenue growth of 37.9%, adjusted EBITDA of $148.8 million (+22.7%), and adjusted net income of $75.2 million (+20.7%) at the midpoint. The updated 2025 targets are much lower than the initial 2025 targets, which was the primary cause of the share price collapsing, and we acknowledge that. However, the company continues to grow.

Analysts expect EPS of $3.45 in 2026 and $4.79 in 2027, implying roughly 38% annual growth. To frame the valuation, let’s run a more conservative scenario.

If EPS grows at just 15% annually (less than half of current forecasts), Propel would have EPS of about $2.88 in 2026 and $3.31 in 2027. Applying a P/E multiple between 8x and 12x suggests a 2027 price range of $26.48 to $39.72. That equates to an annualized return of roughly 11.5% to 36.4%. When factoring in the current 3.8% dividend yield and the potential for continued dividend growth, investors could see a compelling total return and a rising yield on cost over time.

With Propel trading below its one-year average P/E and showing a PEG ratio well under 1x, the market seems to be pricing it as a mature lender despite its strong growth profile. We see that as a disconnect. Given Propel’s early-stage growth trajectory, we think the multiple will eventually re-rate higher, setting up investors with attractive total return potential over a reasonable investment horizon.

Tailwinds That Investors Are Missing

Propel funds its operations through three credit facilities, each tied to a specific business unit. The Fora credit facility in Canada, and the MoneyKey and CreditFresh facilities in the U.S. The CreditFresh facility is by far the most significant, accounting for roughly 95% of total borrowings, and is therefore the key driver of financing costs and earnings impact.

Based on the 3-month SOFR and Propel’s reported all-in borrowing rate, we estimate the spread on the CreditFresh facility is about 7%, down from 7.7% in Q1/2025. That 70-bps reduction on $290.4 million outstanding translates into annualized savings of roughly $2.03 million, or about $0.05 per share. If the rate-cut cycle continues, we think Propel should see further relief in interest expense. Lower rates also ease pressure on its customers, potentially supporting loan performance. Over time, sustained balance sheet strength and financial discipline could allow Propel to negotiate even lower spreads as its credit facilities come up for renewal.

We like that Propel is expanding beyond its core North American (i.e. the United States) operations, adding new growth levers through both geographic diversification and its lending-as-a-service platform.

In September 2024, Propel acquired QuidMarket for $71 million, marking its entry into the U.K. subprime lending market. According to LEK Research, roughly 16–17 million people in the U.K. face a growing need for credit, with an estimated $9.2 billion gap in subprime lending supply. Compared to the U.S., the U.K. market remains underpenetrated, which we think positions Propel well to capture future growth.

This growth can already be seen in Propel's financials. QuidMarket now represents about 8% of Propel’s total revenue in the first three quarters of 2025. Its growth has been impressive as revenue rose from $10.1 million in Q1 to $13.0 million in Q3, an annualized increase of ~66%. For comparison, Propel’s largest segment, the CreditFresh Bank Program (65% of total revenue), has grown 20% year-over-year. We expect continued momentum from the U.K. business to serve as a meaningful tailwind for Propel and will be watching closely for how QuidMarket’s contribution evolves over the next few quarters.

Propel also provides LaaS in the United States where they partner with other lenders by providing the operational and technological backbone, and the other lenders provide the financing. This should be a capital-light, high-margin component of Propel's business. In the first three quarters of 2024, Propel generated 1% of revenue from LaaS or $8.9 million, compared to the first three quarters of 2025, where LaaS has generated 3% of total revenue for Propel or $12.2 million. This represents an increase of 277% year-over-year and from Q1/2025 to Q3/2025, revenue from LaaS has grown at an annualized rate of 236%.

The Dividend & Debt Story

The final catalysts we want to point to for Propel are the dividend outlook and the low debt/equity ratio compared to the industry.

Propel announced an increase to its dividend of 8% relative to Q2/2025 and on a YTD basis, using adjusted earnings per share, the payout ratio for Propel sits at ~27.8% ($0.378 in dividends and adjusted diluted EPS of $1.39). With growth expected to continue and room for an increase in the payout ratio, we believe the dividend will continue to evolve, which presents investors with a great opportunity to potentially lock in a high yield on cost later on.

Relative to the industry, Propel's debt-to-equity ratio is very low:

Company Debt-to-Equity Ratio
Propel (PRL:CA) 121.4%
goeasy (GSY:CA) 386.3%
World Acceptance Corp (WRLD) 180.5%
PRA Group (PRAA) 369.7%
LendingTree (TREE) 339.8%
Upstart Holdings (UPST) 262.4%

Even with such a low D/E ratio relative to the industry, Propel maintains a high ROE and revenue growth that outpaces peers. A low D/E ratio relative to the industry provides comfort when we are evaluating options in the subprime lending market.

Key Risks

The main risks to the Propel story are macro-driven. A weakening economy could push up delinquencies, squeezing margins and slowing growth. Another concern for us is the natural aging of Propel’s loan book, as the portfolio matures and expands, profitability ratios will likely trend lower.

Recent data points highlight some early caution signs. In July 2025, the New York Fed’s Center for Microeconomic Data reported that 23.1% of U.S. credit applications were rejected in June, up from 21.5% in February and the highest level since 2014. That tightening credit backdrop could foreshadow rising strain in the subprime segment, and Propel may be in for more pain today, with the hope they come out of this tomorrow.

Propel’s operating margin after interest expense has averaged 15.3% since early 2024, a healthy level by industry standards. But that margin could compress quickly if credit costs rise. We estimate that provisions for loan losses and related liabilities would need to climb from the current 48.7% of revenue (since 2024) to around 64% before profitability is wiped out. A 15-point increase in provisions is not our base case, but it’s a realistic downside scenario in a recessionary environment, and it may pose issues for Propel.

There’s also a structural risk tied to portfolio composition. In Q3 2025, only 44% of originations came from new customers, meaning 56% were repeat borrowers. These repeat clients typically qualify for lower rates, which translates to thinner margins for Propel. As the repeat borrower share grows, yield compression could start to weigh on profitability and it's one reason we’re keeping a close eye on the company’s annualized revenue yield.

This list is not exhaustive; other risks and factors could impact the investment thesis, such as loss in bank partners, failure of the proprietary AI system that Propel has created for underwriting/approval of credit, and broader economic conditions that could lead to slower growth.

Conclusion

The recent selloff in Propel Holdings is overdone. The company continues to post strong growth numbers, expand internationally, and diversify into capital-light segments like lending-as-a-service, all while maintaining solid margins and a conservative balance sheet relative to industry peers. With a forward P/E below 9x, a PEG ratio under 1x, and a growing dividend backed by a low payout ratio, Propel offers one of the more compelling GARP setups in the subprime space. While macro risks remain, the long-term growth story and potential for multiple re-rating give investors an attractive entry point with a favorable risk-reward profile.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Graham: Strong Fundamentals, But Wait For Valuations To Cool Off
data.description:
Introduction

I last rated Graham Corporation (GHM) a buy after its 3QFY25 results due to GHM’s long-term opportunity in the defense and space segment and a potential margin expansion opportunity. Since then the company has reported solid quarterly earnings with strong new order flows. As of now the FY26 first-half orders now stand at ~1.5-2x compared to the first half new orders in the last 3 years.

This increased pace of new orders is well taken by the market, with the stock delivering a decent 72% return over the 9-month period.

While the stock still has good long-term opportunity and near-term margin expansion opportunity, I believe the stock should consolidate at these levels before moving higher given the valuation multiple expanding to much higher territory. Therefore, I am downgrading the stock from buy to hold.

2Q FY26 updates

Earlier this week GHM reported a mixed set of numbers. The revenue came in at $66 million, beating the consensus estimates by $8.5 million, while the adjusted EPS came in slightly worse than expected at $0.31 per share, a miss of $0.02.

Revenue during the quarter grew 23% YoY, primarily driven by project execution and milestone achievement in the defense segment, while the energy and process segment supported the overall growth. Since the revenue is not the real indicator of underlying demand drivers, I wouldn’t be focusing much on it. In short, the execution of projects remained on track for the quarter.

The real underlying demand driver for GHM is the order intakes and backlog. So far FY2026 is proving to be quite a good year for GHM. The company in total has booked a total of $209 million worth of orders in the first two quarters of FY2026. To put this into perspective, the orders in the first half of FY23, FY24, and FY25 were $132, $104, and $120 million, respectively. The significant surge in orders in the first half of FY26 does signal a strong underlying demand just as the company approaches the peak ordering season in 3Q FY2026.

The new orders during the quarter stood at $83.2 million, which includes orders from all of the end markets. Several major orders include a follow-on contract for the MK48 Mod 7 Heavyweight Torpedo for $25.5 million and $22 million ($15 million in 2Q FY26 and $7 million in 3Q FY2027) worth of new contracts from six leading commercial space launch customers. In addition to these, the company also got smaller aftermarket orders and orders from the energy and process segment, particularly in modular nuclear reactors.

On the profitability front, the company reported a 230 bps YoY decline in gross margins primarily due to a one-time mix shift toward low-margin material receipts and a one-time grant benefit in 2Q 2025. Due to the same reasons, the adjusted EBITDA margin also declined YoY but by a smaller margin of 100 bps.

Capital Allocation

As of the latest quarter, the company has a robust balance sheet with no debt and ~$20 million in cash. This net cash position provides GHM flexibility to invest in organic expansion as well as opportunistic inorganic expansion.

As of now the company is undertaking several projects to expand its production capabilities in order to meet the demand in the high-margin and high-growth space and defense segments. One of the key projects is the Batavia manufacturing facility expansion. The facility is a 30k sq ft advanced manufacturing facility being developed to support the US Navy’s shipbuilding schedule. The project is valued at approximately $17.5 million, of which a significant chunk (~$13.5 million) has been provided by the customer as a grant. As of 2Q FY26 the facility is already operational, and by next quarter, the facility should be ramped up completely.

The completion of this facility would enhance order conversion capabilities of GHM and provide an accelerated execution for the shipbuilding industry, which is struggling with supply chain bottlenecks.

Another key capital project by the company is the new Cryogenic test facility in Jupiter, Florida. The project is being constructed near the company’s P3 Technology facility to complement the already established P3 plant. As of 2Q FY26 the facility remains on track with a projected completion sometime in 3Q FY26 and will start testing customer products in the same quarter. Once the facility becomes operational, the company expects to report new bookings related to the facility.

With respect to capital development toward M&A the company had quite a good track record with the fantastic acquisition of Barber Nichols and P3 Technologies. During the quarter the company added another bolt-on acquisition of Xdot bearing technologies. The acquisition cost the company ~$1.5 million with an annual revenue of ~$1 million. While the size of the acquisition is quite small, the impact of the acquisition could be large. Xdot’s patented foil bearing technology would fill BN’s technology gap in the turbomachinery product category, creating synergistic benefits beyond the headline topline addition.

Outlook

Over the last few years the company has made a significant shift toward higher margins and more stable revenue in the defense and space segment and reduced its dependence on cyclical segments like energy and process. For the long term, management has guided for an annualized organic revenue growth of 8-10%, which should come primarily from the new defense and space segments, given management’s focus in these segments.

Especially with respect to the defense segment, there remains to be a substantial demand from the US navy program like the Virginia-class submarine program, the Columbia-class submarine program, and the MK48 Mod 7 Heavyweight Torpedo Program.

While defense is a large growth opportunity, the shipbuilding industry is going through its own set of challenges. The US shipbuilding industry has been facing a steady and long-term decline, and over the last few years, it has been hard for the industry to retain and train the existing workforce. The lack of a skilled workforce has led to delays in many of the deliveries and cost overruns. The delays made it harder for the US navy to maintain or increase their current fleet of vessels. Furthermore, various single-source suppliers for various critical inputs to the vessels have been struggling, creating delays in project completions.

To tackle these supply chain problems, shipbuilders like Huntington Ingalis (HII) and General Dynamics (GD) are taking several measures like retaining, training, and hiking wages for the workforce; increasing outsourcing; and acquiring strategic assets for manufacturing expansion. Furthermore, the government is also helping the industry to fast-track the manufacturing process by allocating ~$11 billion under the Submarine Industrial Base (SIB) and Maritime Industrial Base (MIB) programs.

While the supply chain bottleneck creates a headwind for GHM for order intake, it also acts as an opportunity for the company. Given the stretched-out supply chain in the US navy shipbuilding industry, GHM can get into adjacent product categories and bid for equipment that has a stretched supply chain.

Furthermore, improvement in these supply chain delays would also benefit GHM as it would mean more orders for the company. Slowly these issues are being resolved. Going forward I expect these challenges to improve and result in accelerated order booking.

Even now GHM’s order book has shown quite strong growth during the last 2-3 quarters, with the most order-heavy quarter of the year still pending. One could expect this trend to even strengthen going forward.

Similarly, the space segment also provides a long-term growth opportunity. The space industry is now moving beyond scientific exploration toward value-added space asset developments. This transition should significantly aid the growth for the segment.

Overall the company has a solid long-term growth runway, and it would be highly probable for the company to achieve its long-term growth target of 8-10% revenue growth. In the near term, ~$500 million worth of order backlog guarantees revenue for GHM for the next 1-2 years.

With respect to margins, there is scope for near-term margin expansion due to the completion of the BN earnout bonus. The BN earnout was a 3-year program spanning from FY24 to FY26. As of FY25 the payout was ~$4.3 million, which resulted in a headwind of ~203 bps to adjusted EBITDA. After the completion of this earnout, the adjusted EBITDA margin should retreat back higher. As per management, they are expecting a 13%-15% adjusted EBITDA margin starting from FY27 from the current 11% guided in FY26.

Valuations

GHM currently trades at ~27.3x forward (FY26) EV/EBITDA which seems quite expensive if we compare it to the industry average of just 12.2x. If we look at the last 3-4 years of the historical EV/EBITDA range, the stock is currently trending near the higher end of the range, showcasing recent optimism regarding the recent new order flows.

I believe the stock has a strong long-term outlook from here, as well as a near-term fundamental momentum that also looks to be in favor of the company. However, the current forward FY26 27.3x EV/EBITDA seems to be quite stretched to me. Instead, I have used a little lower multiple (22x) in my fair value estimates. These are multiple factors in the potential ramp-up in new orders in the near term as well as the long-term opportunity in the stock. Furthermore, I have also estimated a ramp-up in EBITDA margin following the completion of the BN earnout. Still, the company offers little upside from the current valuation.

Conclusion

While GHM has strong near- as well as long-term fundamentals and a possibility of new order ramp-up in the near future, I believe current valuation multiples have room to cool off. Therefore, I am downgrading the stock from buy to hold.

data.title: Tempus: Diagnostics Substance, AI Flash
data.description:
Back in March of this year, we talked at length about applications of AI to the drug discovery process in the context of Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX). During that conversation, we mentioned another AI healthcare name in passing that has stuck in our collective craw as of late: Tempus AI (TEM). Unsurprisingly, the “AI” in the name is a recent addition to what is otherwise a very standard molecular diagnostics startup from a serial entrepreneur. As one of Cathie Wood’s larger calls, about 5x larger than Recursion, and the largest healthcare name of ARK’s combined holdings, we wanted to continue our scrutiny of applied AI in the space we know best, healthcare.

The result of our analysis is pretty much what we'd expect: a tech-forward diagnostics business looking to make good on the network effect they've built up through all the testing they perform, especially in oncology, to break into a more scalable and hopefully lucrative business model focused on AI and "software as a medical device". That "zoom-out pivot", while it did their stock price some favors, has not translated into the kind of inflection in their financials folks may have been led to believe, which is enough for us to recommend steering clear until the "AI hype" dies down in earnest.

Overview: Molecular Diagnostics At-A-Glance

The life sciences community was all abuzz back in the early 2010s about “personalized medicine” with the advent of “big data” and the lowered barriers to access high-quality “next-gen” sequencing technologies. That whole hype cycle diffused out without much to show for it on the broader market, except maybe a handful of drugs where genetic testing could determine the patient’s expected responsiveness. Tempus came on to the scene against this backdrop in 2015, with their core business primarily in what is known as “molecular diagnostics”. These kinds of diagnostic tests involve analyzing patient samples for differences at the molecular level, such as the specific mutations (or lack thereof) in the genome of a cancerous tumor that can provide valuable clues to an oncologist on which treatments will have the highest likelihood of success.

Given the gravity of the oncology field and the high-stakes, high-cost nature of some of the treatments used in late-line therapies (think immune checkpoint treatments, like PD-1 inhibitors), an oncologist (and/or an insurance company) might not mind (or may even mandate) paying 100-fold less the cost of a treatment cycle (i.e. - $100k vs $1k) to have the tumor’s genome mapped and increase the confidence that said expensive treatment will actually work. On the flip side, Tempus gets to keep the data that they collect as a part of the diagnostics process, now having compiled a trove of genomic tumor data, the likes of which are endlessly useful for all types of oncological research and development and which Tempus can now license out to interested parties for a nominal fee.

At present, there are two big names in agreements with Tempus, AstraZeneca (AZN) and GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), and one smaller name, Recursion, so small they’ve actually paid their licensing fees in stock instead of cash. In fact, Tempus has even gone so far as to suggest they can “rent” these data sets out to clients for their AI/ML workflows, a concept that eerily echoes the growing trend in the tech industry of end users “own[ing] nothing and be[ing] happy”.

This part of the business appears healthy and productive; one attribute to which we want to call attention is the inorganic nature of its growth. In ten years of business, Tempus has made no fewer than seven acquisitions, at least five of which were prior to their IPO. At the end of 2024, this didn’t seem like that big of a deal, with Goodwill consisting of a little under 8% of total assets; after the acquisition of Ambry Genetics, however, that number has jumped to around 20% of total assets thanks to a nearly $230M goodwill charge from the deal, along with a spike in the “Other Intangibles” on the books (which can likely also be traced back to a nearly $234M “Client Relationships” line-item from the Ambry deal):

An acquisition of this size seems to suggest that the company is ready to move up a proverbial “weight class” and start turbocharging the clinic-to-data-set pipeline; our concern is that this may be premature and that Tempus as a parent company may not have the infrastructure in place it needs to be able to support these subsidiaries. Additionally, this big of a deal may indicate that Tempus’ AI aspirations require larger and larger infusions of data in order to show incremental improvements in model performance.

The Proposed AI Value-Add

And speaking of AI aspirations, the rhetoric of this “AI era” seems to suggest that AI is going to be a “step function” of performance, quality and efficiency practically overnight, and Tempus’ management is no exception. However, the reality of what AI is actually doing makes that aspirational commentary ring a little hollow.

To set the stage a bit, AI-powered “algorithms,” as management refers to their offerings, are basically an extension of the molecular diagnostics paradigm to other fields of medicine: Perform an otherwise “routine” diagnostic test such as an ECG, sonogram, or MRI, then pipe the results through an AI system and get results as good as a more thorough (but possibly more expensive or invasive) examination for way less cost and way less disruption to the patient. At the moment, Tempus has two FDA-approved AI-powered algorithms, under what the FDA classifies as “software as a medical device,” and both are cardiological in nature – predictions of signs of either atrial fibrillation (AFib or AF) or low left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF).

Interestingly, these approved algorithms are not “novel” medical devices as far as the FDA is concerned; they were filed under the “510(k)” option, claiming they are “substantially similar” to another on-market device. The similarities trace back to a machine learning algorithm developed by another AI-powered healthcare company, Viz.ai, approved as a “de novo” medical device in 2023 and used to detect hypertrophic cardiomyopathy based on ECGs. In the case of low LVEF, the similarity is to a test from a third AI-powered healthcare startup, Anumana, more focused on AI-powered cardiology solutions, which itself was approved via 510(k) to Viz's HCM test.

Reading through the FDA filings, what’s interesting to note is that while the sensitivity (true positive rate) and selectivity (true negative rate) are both targeted at 80% for the tests, the actual predictive value of the tests skews towards negatives. Perhaps because the sample sizes are so disparate, the targeted sensitivity and specificity are usually in the range they need to be, but the negative and positive “predictive value” (i.e. - how likely is a positive or negative result correct) tell a much different story. All of the tests we reviewed had negative predictive values of nearly 98-99% and positive predictive values less than 50%.

Our read on these results is that the tests are much better at excluding candidates versus identifying them, hence the repeated warnings that the results not be used in a diagnosis or as the basis for starting or stopping treatment for a particular condition. Tempus explains the 19% positive predictive value of their AFib test in the FDA filing thusly:

The observed 19% PPV can be interpreted in the context of the baseline 1-year incidence rate of AF (6%) observed in the study population as follows: if the intended use population were clinically evaluated for AF, AF would be observed in approximately 1-in-16 patients within the next 1 year. For those receiving a positive result from the test, AF would be observed in approximately 1-in-5 patients within the next 1 year. (Tempus 510(k) Summary K233549)

On the surface, this all makes sense: the software is not perfect, but at least it helps to reduce the patient load to something more manageable for a healthcare system under ever-increasing pressure from an aging population. These, however, are just incremental improvements, nothing so revolutionary as we’ve been primed to expect from the AI whisperers.

The most recent earnings call echoes the unusually hushed attitude from leadership about the state of their AI diagnostics and reimbursement from payers. After CEO Erik Lefkofsky gave a surprisingly short opening remark compared to other earnings calls we usually listen to or read, in which his comments focused mostly on the Genomics division, analysts asked ten questions, only one of which was directly related to reimbursement for data-based or “dry lab” diagnostics. In response, Lefkofsky reiterated his talking points from previous earnings calls about the need for disruptive change in the healthcare system and how “AI algorithms” may be the only way out of “this mess,” as he called it.

He sounded hopeful that AI algorithms will scale more easily in terms of deployment and revenue versus traditional diagnostics and that said revenue scalability would flow through to the income statement much more quickly as a result, but those that actually deploy IT in healthcare settings and DevOps software professionals may take that projection with a grain of salt. All-in-all, though, the analyst sentiment seems to be discounting the contribution of any new AI-powered diagnostics to the overall business for the foreseeable future, and we concur with that assessment.

Management and Ownership

The latest earnings call is a good segue into discussing the leadership and ownership story in more depth. It wouldn’t surprise us if Tempus’ ability to attract interest and capital is directly attributable to their veritable “rockstar” of a CEO, Erik Lefkofsky. Lefkofsky’s track record is hard to argue with, having successfully infused tech into traditional businesses several times over, to the tune of a couple billion dollars’ net worth. Hand-in-hand with him for this entire journey is the venture capital fund New Enterprise Associates (NEA), who unsurprisingly seeded Tempus and have two partners on the board of directors. However, NEA has exited their position in Tempus, according to their own website.

The rest of the board is a typical list of biotech-aware individuals such as MDs and PhDs, including Nobel Laureate Jennifer Doudna; and a considerable number of MBAs with experience in the healthcare sector, along with, strangely enough, the owner of a number of sports and entertainment ventures in the Washington, DC area, connected to Lefkofsky through his crown jewel, Groupon (GRPN). Also of note is that no board member has Big Four or CFO experience, something we saw previously with Recursion’s board, but with several CEOs and a finance MBA on the board for several years, it doesn’t feel as “in the air” as it did when we looked at Recursion.

What jumps out at us more than anything, though, is the fact that Lefkofsky and NEA have gone and founded another AI-powered healthcare startup, Pathos, taking aim at AI-powered drug discovery. They disclose “related-party transactions” in Tempus’ latest 10-Q concerning a three-way deal with Pathos and AstraZeneca, with Pathos utilizing Tempus’ data and compute to build an oncological “foundational model”, for which Tempus will be receiving cash and a license to use the model (and the right to sub-license the model to AstraZeneca). It makes sense to some extent that AstraZeneca would broker the deal through Tempus instead of with Pathos directly, given they already have a licensing arrangement for Tempus’ data and that Tempus being public holds them to a more rigorous standard of reporting and accounting. All said, the concern for Tempus is that everyone involved sees them as just their “diagnostics-to-data-set” pipeline and not much more, and the capital earmarked for AI innovation has been redistributed to drug discovery efforts.

Financials and Valuation

Despite Lefkofsky’s claim that the business is now “adjusted EBITDA positive,” they are still not turning a profit, and with the frigid feelings about AI algorithm prospects in the near-term, we have to value them as primarily a molecular diagnostics and data licensing business. One feature we found interesting in reading through their financial statements is the rather convoluted financing arrangements they’ve made, including:

  • Issuing convertible debt and promptly taking out a “capped call” with some of the creditors to minimize the dilutive effect of a large-scale conversion event

  • Entering into a credit arrangement with Ares Capital (ARCC) that helped to finance the Ambry deal but also has a “step-up” in the term loan interest rate starting next year, alongside an option to pay-in-kind part of the interest if necessary

  • A “convertible promissory note” to Google (GOOG) for which the principal is reduced based on how much Tempus spends on Google Cloud Platform resources, an arrangement to ensure loyalty for Google and cheap(er) financing for Tempus at the cost of some vendor lock-in

The financing deals and the large acquisitions have definitely started to manifest in the company’s debt to long-term capital ratio, a number we watch very closely as a measure of financial health. When compared to two pure-play molecular diagnostics names of similar size, Qiagen (QGEN) and Illumina (ILMN), Tempus’ leverage is worrying:

If we compare the three companies on price-to-sales (since Tempus is not making a profit yet), Tempus’ valuation is, in a word, lofty:

It’s also worth noting that Illumina is still picking up the pieces of their attempts to reacquire Grail (GRAL), which has them well off of their 2021 highs but still better-capitalized than most in the space.

With a price-to-sales nearly double that of its peers in molecular diagnostics, the natural next question to ask is: Are the prospects of Tempus’ AI-powered diagnostics actually worth double the per-dollar sales of names like Qiagen and Illumina? In our opinion, no, they are not. That immediately drops the “worst-case” valuation to basically 40% of their current price, around $30/share, but that’s probably overly pessimistic, a “salvage value” valuation, even with goodwill and intangibles factored in. At this point, for an AI-first healthcare business, the closest point of comparison would be a name like SOPHiA Genetics (SOPH), with a price-to-sales of around 4. That gives us a present value of about $15/share for their AI prospects at this moment, for an overall present valuation of $45/share.

Conclusion

Suffice to say, the AI part of this narrative is taking longer to materialize than most anyone involved considered: most AI-powered algorithms are being explored by other small startups and are mostly centered around cardiology, analysts aren’t asking questions about the AI-powered algorithms, and the CEO and VC stakeholders have migrated to a different AI-powered healthcare challenge. Meanwhile, the insatiable desire of machine learning models for larger and larger data sets is starting to play out with Tempus, as their acquisitions continue to get bigger and the financing they need to pull off those deals grows in kind.

This business feels like another one caught up in the stark reality of building any sort of foundational-level deep learning model, where the data and compute costs are steeper upfront and longer to see a return on than anyone anticipates. If the hype really dies down and AI prospects come back to something closer to a fair valuation, they may be worth a flyer thanks to their steady Genomics and Data businesses, but otherwise, this company feels like one to avoid for the time being.

data.title: MGP Ingredients Is Cheap For A Reason
data.description:
To be honest with you, I am a teetotaler. I could probably put all of the alcohol I have ever consumed in my life into a large wine bottle, and I don't know if that bottle would be full. Having said that, I do find this industry to be interesting. This includes the whiskey market that companies like MGP Ingredients (MGPI) thrive in. What makes this business particularly interesting is just how cheap shares are. But even though I am a value investor with a contrarian streak, even I acknowledge that sometimes, unfortunately, the market gets things right. Given the uncertainty that exists here in light of changing consumer preferences and industry conditions, I cannot rate MGP Ingredients anything higher than a ‘hold’ right now, which matches what I rated it in the past.

I’m not downing this drink

The last few years have been a pretty bumpy ride for shareholders of MGP Ingredients. From 2022 to 2023, revenue for the company jumped from $782.4 million to $836.5 million. But then, in 2024, sales plunged to $703.6 million. This decline in revenue was broad based, with all three of the firm's operating segments experiencing a decline. The Branded Spirits segment of the business, for instance, reported a decline in revenue from $253.9 million to $240.8 million. This was in spite of the fact that the company benefited to the tune of 2% from a favorable change in pricing and product mix. The problem, then, was a 7% hit to volume.

According to management, this was at least somewhat intentional. You see, management made the deliberate decision to hike prices, including on its lower margin mid and value brands. Those who buy these are certainly price sensitive. So the end result was a decline in volume that management fully intended for with the hopes of improvements on the bottom line. The good news is that we did see that improvement. Segment profits actually expanded from $112.8 million in 2023 to $118.2 million last year. And for context, back in 2022, segment profits were only $95.5 million.

We also saw a bit of a decline when it came to the Ingredient Solutions business, but that is hardly worth mentioning. As prices and changes in product mix negatively affected revenue by 5%, volumes grew by 4%. The end result here was a dip in sales from $131.7 million to $130.6 million. Sadly, however, profitability took a huge hit, with gross profits contracting from $47 million to $26.2 million. This was blamed on the removal of an intercompany credit for its waste starch slurry byproduct that the company missed out on because of its decision to close its Atchison Distillery, and also because of greater input costs and higher expenses associated with its new extrusion manufacturing facility.

The big driver of the revenue decline, however, was neither of these. Instead, it was the company’s Distilling Solutions segment. After rising from $428.5 million in 2022 to $450.9 million in 2023, revenue plunged to just $332.2 million last year. This was in spite of the fact that the company benefited from higher volumes of 5%. And it was driven by a 13% slam that the company endured because of changes in pricing and product mix. Softening in the American whiskey market and rising industry inventories created this pain. In some cases, the company even endured customer contract performance lapses.

This touches on some major problems for the company. For years, growing demand for American whiskey had caused distillers to build new capacity. Some of this was because of higher domestic sales. But it was also because of a rise in exports. But now, younger drinkers are shifting away from whiskey. This $9 billion market, when focused just on Kentucky, is already experiencing pressure. In fact, just this year alone, three large Kentucky distilleries entered into bankruptcy. And this comes at a time when younger generations, particularly members of Gen Z, are transitioning out of bourbon and into ready to drink canned cocktails and lighter alcoholic beverages such as hard seltzers. This overall trend is finally coming to a head, but it's not exactly new. Back in the early 2000s, 72% of young Americans drank alcohol. But today, that number is around 62%. And in fact, Gen Z is consuming 20% less alcohol on a per capita basis than millennials did at the same age.

This overall decline in revenue has brought with it lower profits. As the first chart in this article illustrates, cash flows have been fairly consistent. But overall net income has fallen. This year, things are looking even worse. Revenue for the first nine months of 2025 came out to $398.1 million. That's a whopping 23.9% lower than the $522.8 million that the company reported a year earlier. Some of this pain can be chalked up to the Branded Spirits segment, with revenue dropping from $176.8 million to $169.5 million as volumes dropped by 2% and prices and product mix contributed to another 2% decline. The Ingredient Solutions segment saw a drop in revenue as well, with sales collapsing from $95.9 million to $90.7 million. In this case, the hit was entirely driven by lower volumes amounting to five per cent that management blamed primarily on adverse weather conditions and the aforementioned distillery closure. The commercialization of new specialty wheat protein customers also proved problematic, though to a lesser extent it seems.

The real issue, once again, was the Distilling Solutions part of the company. Revenue plunged from $250.2 million to $137.8 million. Brown goods sales were decimated, falling from $198.9 million to $97.2 million. The major problem here was a 44% collapse in volume, though the 7% hit associated with changes in price and product mix certainly didn't help. At the end of the day, however, customer demand showed weakness while elevated industry wide barrel inventory levels exacerbated the situation.

With the overall drop in sales for the company, profitability declined also. Net income was slashed from $75.8 million to $26.5 million. We saw operating cash flow grow from $73.5 million to $92.8 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a substantial fall from $109.3 million to $73.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $143.4 million to $89.9 million.

When it comes to this year in its entirety, management anticipates revenue of only $525 million to $535 million. However, there is some good news from a profitability standpoint. As the image above illustrates, overall profitability, while it will be lower than it was last year, is forecasted to come in higher than what management anticipated earlier this year. This is a testament to management's ability to address cost issues during these difficult times. And with the company still undergoing a strategic review in an effort to try and generate as much value for shareholders as possible, there is the prospect of additional savings as time goes on.

If we use the estimates provided by management for this year and project out results based on what we have seen already when it comes to adjusted operating cash flow, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Normally, these multiples would be hard to resist. And in fact, as the table below illustrates, MGP Ingredients is trading cheaper than most other similar enterprises. But to be perfectly honest with you, this decline in revenue and profits scares me. The fact of the matter is that while there could be an opportunity here, I really don't see industry conditions recovering all that much. Admittedly, the excess inventory will eventually work itself out. That's not something I'm worried about long term. What I am worried about is the shift in consumer preferences. That is something that is difficult to correct if it is correctable at all. And when you add on top of that the inventory issues that exist for the industry, you create a lot of uncertainty.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
MGP Ingredients 5.3 6.9
Constellation Brands (STZ) 8.5 9.1
Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.A) (BF.B) 17.1 13.0
Diageo (DEO) 11.8 12.1
Pernod Ricard SA (OTCPK:PRNDY) 12.8 10.3
Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) 8.2 6.3

Takeaway

Conceptually, I want to be bullish about MGP Ingredients. As a value investor with a contrarian streak, this is the kind of opportunity that I live for. But I have to see a light at the end of the tunnel, and that is not something I see here. Perhaps management would be successful with their strategic review. Maybe they will sell the company or merge it with somebody else. Maybe they will engage in some major asset sale aimed at boosting shareholder value. At some point, industry conditions will stabilize. But the demand side of the equation will likely remain challenged for a long time. Because of this, I think that a ‘hold’ rating is the most generous rating that I can give the company at this time.

data.title: Nvidia Teed Up For A Strong Q3 Earnings Report (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is set to report Q3 ’26 earnings on November 19, 2025 after market close. Despite the recent headlines of SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) selling its entire $5.83b stake in NVDA shares, I have reason to believe that Nvidia is set up to report significant growth with a durable growth trajectory for the final quarter of eFY26. Given the optimistic outlook, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating with a price target of $351/share at 26.62x eFY27 price/sales.

You can review my previous coverage of Nvidia here:

“Nvidia Is Still In The Early Innings Of The AI Revolution.”

Nvidia Earnings Expectations

Nvidia received significant support during the calendar year calendar year Q3 ’25 earnings cycle, particularly driven by the major hyperscalers. The biggest takeaway received from the recent earnings reports was the increased spend for compute capacity going into the end of 2025 and into 2026. In total, the 3 major cloud services providers [CSPs], including Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN) invested an aggregate $267b in the last twelve months, largely directed towards data center, network infrastructure, and compute capacity [CPUs & GPUs].

  • Microsoft: $78b estimated capital spend for eCY25 [fiscal year q3’25-q2’26].
  • Alphabet: $92.6b estimated capital spend for eFY25.
  • Amazon: $125.8b estimated capital spend for eFY25.
  • Meta Platforms: $71.85b estimated capital spend for eFY25.

Total capital outlay: $368.25b.

In addition to this, management outlined increased capital deployment for 2026, largely for sourcing additional compute capacity to support the heightened demand for cloud services.

With accelerating demand and a growing RPO balance, we're increasing our spend on GPUs and CPUs. Therefore, total spend will increase sequentially, and we now expect the FY '26 growth rate to be higher than FY '25.

Amy Hood, EVP & CFO, Microsoft.

You're going to see us continue to be very aggressive investing in capacity because we see the demand. As fast as we're adding capacity right now, we're monetizing it. It's still quite early and represents an unusual opportunity for customers in AWS.

Andrew Jassy, President, CEO, & Director, Amazon.

We're continuing to invest aggressively due to the demand we're experiencing from Cloud customers as well as the growth opportunities we see across the company… Looking out to 2026, we expect a significant increase in CapEx.

Anat Ashkenazi, SVP & CFO, Alphabet.

Granted, investors must bear in mind that not all capital deployed will go towards Nvidia GPUs, particularly as Alphabet and Amazon will leverage their internally developed chips to build out additional capacity. Specifically, Alphabet is expecting to utilize its Ironwood TPUs to build out its 1GW data center as part of a partnership with Anthropic (ANTHRO). Amazon, in a similar fashion, will be deploying upwards of 1mm Trainium2 chips for the data center developed in partnership with Anthropic.

Net of these two major buildouts, I have reason to believe Nvidia’s GPUs will remain as a critical component for new AI infrastructure. An additional factor investors must consider is that the hyperscalers may be entering or nearing a refresh cycle for the first generation of AI infrastructure capacity in the coming year. Management at Microsoft hinted in the Q1 ’26 earnings call that aging GPUs will need to be replaced. Investors must bear in mind that OpenAI launched ChatGPT at the end of November 2023, a mere 2 years ago. With data center GPUs expected to run for 1-3 years, the CSPs are likely entering into the first refresh cycle as it pertains to the AI business. With this in mind, I believe that the CSPs will not only need to invest in growth capital but also begin investing in maintenance capital for AI infrastructure, potentially compounding the investment outlay for the coming years.

Supporting growth investments is the cumulative backlog of $1.21T across the CSPs, including Oracle Corp. (ORCL). Investors should bear in mind that this is loosely tied to AI infrastructure as cloud hosting services will also cover general compute capacity. Nonetheless, the growing backlog supports the investment outlay for adding compute capacity at an increasing rate for the next fiscal year.

In terms of Nvidia’s business in China, Colette Kress, EVP & CFO of Nvidia, indicated at the Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology conference 2025 that Nvidia has received licenses for several customers in China to ship H20 GPUs. On November 11, 2025, the Wall Street Journal reported that Chinese chip production may be facing certain setbacks that may solidify Nvidia’s dominance in the market. One of the factors included the challenges Chinese foundries face with less advanced manufacturing systems producing lower quality chips.

In the report, Morgan Stanley analysts noted that manufacturing Huawei’s 910C chips using SMIC’s technology may result in only 5% of chips being of quality. The WSJ report also indicated that engineers in China are facing challenges using alternative chips to Nvidia’s technology, including “overheating, system crashes, and lack of software support.” This may be a positive sign for Nvidia re-ramping up business in the country following the trade restrictions for Chinese sales.

Nvidia Financial Outlook

Looking to Q3 ’26, I’m forecasting Nvidia to generate $58b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.33/share. I incrementally raised my revenue guidance from my previous report covering Nvidia given the recent Q3 ’25 earnings report by Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which presented stronger Gaming & Client segment revenue for the quarter. I believe this should translate to strength in the segment for Nvidia, despite being a relatively smaller market segment when taking into consideration the size of the Data Center segment.

Given the updated guidance for selling to the Chinese market, I believe Nvidia may realize stronger revenue for eFY27 as the firm ramps up production of the H20 architecture.

Risks Related to Nvidia

Bull Case

Nvidia remains the leader for AI accelerators, posing a dominant position in both the U.S. and China [in terms of technology, as suggested by the report in the WSJ]. The investment cycle for compute capacity remains durable, particularly as a refresh cycle may be imminent.

Bear Case

The hyperscalers are heavily investing in custom ASICs, which are expected to be deployed for Anthropic’s data center capacity.

NVDA shares are currently trading at a relatively rich premium at 28.79x price/sales, potentially exposing investors to certain risks in the upcoming quarter. If management’s outlook for Q4 ’26 is relatively lackluster, shares may experience a significant decline.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

November 21, 2025 options trading on NVDA shares is showing some hedging going into the earnings call with significant puts volumes being traded at $185, potentially suggesting investors are anticipating a pullback in the share price.

Despite options positions, I believe NVDA shares are trading somewhat neutral as investors await the Q3 ’25 earnings call on November 19th.

Fundamentally, I believe NVDA shares should be valued at $351/share at 26.62x eFY27 price/sales, the target scenario in the valuation table below. Given the strong catalysts at hand, I believe Nvidia will be in a position to realize continued top-line growth for the coming years, particularly as the hyperscalers build out large-scale AI data centers to support the growing demand. I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for NVDA shares.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: SSK: When Staking Drives Dividends
data.description:
Introduction

The SSK REX-Osprey SOL + Staking ETF (SSK), is an option for those seeking exposure to the Solana cryptocurrency and at the same time obtain monthly dividends derived from staking.

Next, the main thread of the article will be to show in such a way that the reader understands what staking is and how the fund generates cash to distribute monthly through this methodology. Similarly, we will compare it with other market options and we will finish by analyzing the current correction phase.

Holdings: Know What You Own

Currently the fund has three Solana formats in its portfolio: firstly, 53.15% are holdings of the currency, 43.86% correspond to CoinShares which are acquired shares of CoinShares Physical Staked Solana ETP (SLNC) , 5.16% correspond to First American Government Obligs and finally, the fourth way (2.85%) is LSD (Liquid Staking Derivative) from JitoSOL.

This is important because the fund has 3 different ways to obtain returns through staking. But, before delving into how it is generated, let's see what staking is.

Staking & Validators

If you are not familiar with the blockchain world and without entering into technicalities or vocabulary “on-chain” staking consists of a simple way, to deposit an asset for a flexible (can be withdrawn at any time) or fixed term (there is a commitment of time for which it must remain deposited) and in return, receive an interest receive interest in the same asset (i.e., Petar deposits 10 solanas on Platform B and commits to maintain them for two years, in exchange, he receives X% of solanas Y every time). Such operations are very common in the DeFi industry.

As additional information, the concept of staking was born with networks with protocol PoS (Proof Of Stake) in which its main characteristic is that validators, responsible for processing transactions and critical part in the security and operation of a blockchain, have the obligation or requirement to deposit for a period of time Y (renewable) an X amount of the native cryptocurrency in staking to be able to exercise their functions.

However, it remarkable to highlight an important nuance: this total amount X is not only the validator’s own stake and it also includes delegated stake, but it (the “delegated part”) must be added and this is where portfolio management becomes critical: it hinges on the routes the fund takes to extract yield from SOL. Depending on the setup, the manager may select validators manually or rely on an automated selection/rebalancing mechanism, but we’ll discuss this process later.

In reward for the operational work of validating and processing transactions and for locking their coins/tokens, they receive a certain amount of the native currency for each transaction processed. Add that not only does this type of deposits exist, but in addition to the staking of validators, within each blockchain protocol, other ways of staking, lending, farming, liquid staking, etc. have been developed. In what is known as the world of decentralized finance: DeFi.

Without going too far into technicalities, delegation (which the fund does with part of its SOL) is a mechanism through which any entity that has solanas in its possession can interact with this program to participate in the staking, as if it were another validator. This is what the vehicle does directly with 2.85% of its total assets and indirectly with 43.86% (since CoinShares runs its own delegation mechanisms). In this way, validators also compete with each other to provide attractive conditions for raising capital.
